title: AI Pair Programming — The Copilot Awakens

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you'll set up GitHub Copilot and experience inline completions and Copilot Chat first-hand.

The guide panel will update automatically as you advance through the slides. The glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look.

steps.title: Install the Extension
steps.instruction:
We've opened the Extensions panel for you (Ctrl+Shift+X). Since VS Code 1.96, Copilot ships built into VS Code; just sign in with your GitHub account. If GitHub Copilot is not pre-installed, you can search for it here and click Install.

All features (completions, chat, agent mode) are in the single GitHub Copilot extension.

steps.title: Check the Extension Details
steps.instruction:
Click on the GitHub Copilot extension in the list to open its details page. Scroll through to see what's included:

• Inline completions (ghost text)
• Copilot Chat panel and inline chat
• Terminal integration

Notice the Disable and Uninstall buttons. You won't need those today, but it's good to know where they are.

steps.title: Verify the Status Bar
steps.instruction:
Look at the bottom-right corner of VS Code. You should see a small Copilot icon (a sparkle) in the status bar.

If it's visible and not greyed out, the extension is active. Click it to see quick actions like signing in, checking status, or disabling Copilot temporarily.

steps.title: Close Extensions
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Extensions panel for you. You explored the Copilot extension, verified it's installed, and checked the status bar icon.

You can reopen Extensions any time with Ctrl+Shift+X.

steps.title: Sign In to GitHub
steps.instruction:
Click the Copilot icon in the status bar → Sign in to GitHub. Authorize VS Code in your browser, then come back here.

The icon should turn into the Copilot sparkle when you're active.

steps.title: Verify Your License
steps.instruction:
Click the Copilot status bar icon in the bottom-right corner of VS Code to check your activation status.

This confirms whether Copilot is connected and your license is active. If it reports a problem, check your organization seat assignment or try signing out and back in.

steps.title: Check the Output Logs
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel (Ctrl+Shift+U) and select GitHub Copilot from the dropdown.

This is where Copilot logs connection events and errors. If you ever run into trouble, this panel is your first stop for diagnosing issues.
steps.tip: Bookmark this in your mind: the Output panel is the single best debugging tool for Copilot connectivity problems.

steps.title: Close the Panel
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Output panel for you. You signed into GitHub, verified your license, and found where Copilot logs connection events.

If you ever hit a connectivity issue, bring the Output panel back with Ctrl+Shift+U and select GitHub Copilot from the dropdown.

steps.title: Try Inline Completions
steps.instruction:
We've opened a new file for you. Set the language to JavaScript using the language selector in the bottom-right corner, then type this comment:

// Calculate the total price of items in a shopping cart

Press Enter and watch Copilot suggest the function body as ghost text. Press Tab to accept the full suggestion.

steps.title: Accept Word-by-Word
steps.instruction:
Now try a different example. Below your first function, type:

// Validate an email address and return true or false

When the ghost text appears, instead of pressing Tab, press Ctrl+→ repeatedly. Each press accepts one word at a time — perfect when the suggestion is mostly right but you want to tweak as you go.
steps.tip: This is the most underused shortcut in Copilot. Once you learn it, you'll use it constantly.

steps.title: Cycle Through Alternatives
steps.instruction:
Try one more. Type:

// Reverse a string without using built-in reverse methods

When ghost text appears, press Alt+] to see the next alternative and Alt+[ for the previous one. Copilot often has multiple approaches — a for-loop, a reduce, a spread-and-reverse. Explore them before accepting.

steps.title: Dismiss and Redirect
steps.instruction:
Type a new comment:

// Shuffle an array randomly

When ghost text appears, press Esc to dismiss it. Now start typing the function signature manually: function shuffle(arr) {

Notice how Copilot generates a different suggestion once you've given it more context. The more structure you provide, the better the result.

steps.title: Next Edit Suggestions
steps.instruction:
Next Edit Suggestions (NES) take inline completions further. After you accept a suggestion, look for a dimmed suggestion at a different location in your file. Copilot predicts where you’ll want to edit next.

Try it: accept a ghost text suggestion with Tab, then look for another suggestion nearby. If you see one, press Tab again to jump to it and accept.
steps.tip: NES works best when you’re making a series of related edits, like adding parameters to a function and then updating all the call sites.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction: We've opened the Copilot Chat panel for you (Ctrl+Alt+I). You'll see the chat interface on the right side of your screen.

steps.title: Choose Your Model
steps.instruction:
Click the model selector dropdown at the top of the Chat panel. Different models have different strengths — Claude excels at reasoning, GPT-4o is fast and versatile.

For this lab, any model works. Pick one and continue.

steps.title: Ask a Context-Aware Question
steps.instruction:
With the file you wrote earlier still open, type:

How could I improve the functions in my current file?

Notice that Copilot sees your open file and gives specific feedback — not generic advice. This context awareness is what makes Chat so much more useful than a web search.

steps.title: Try Inline Chat
steps.instruction:
Click inside one of your functions in the editor, then press Ctrl+I to open inline chat — a small input box right in the editor.

Type: Add input validation

Inline chat is perfect for quick, targeted edits without switching to the sidebar.
steps.tip: <kbd>Ctrl+Alt+I</kbd> opens the sidebar panel. <kbd>Ctrl+I</kbd> opens inline chat. Two different tools for two different jobs.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Find the Model Selector
steps.instruction:
Look at the top of the Chat panel. You’ll see a dropdown showing the current model name (e.g., GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet).

Click it to open the model selector.

steps.title: Browse Available Models
steps.instruction:
The model selector shows all available models grouped by provider. Notice the multiplier next to each model; this tells you how many premium requests each message consumes.

A 1× model uses one request per message; a higher multiplier uses more. Choose based on the task: fast models for quick questions, reasoning models for complex problems.

steps.title: Switch and Compare
steps.instruction:
Select a different model from the dropdown. Now ask a question you asked earlier:

How could I improve the functions in my current file?

Notice how different models may give different suggestions, levels of detail, or coding styles. There is no single "best" model; it depends on the task.

steps.title: Done with Models
steps.instruction: You’ve explored the model selector, browsed providers, and compared responses. Remember: you can switch models at any time, even mid-conversation.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Explain Your Code
steps.instruction:
Select one of the functions you wrote earlier, then type in the chat input:

/explain

Copilot breaks down how the code works step by step — great for understanding unfamiliar code or verifying your own logic.

steps.title: Fix a Problem
steps.instruction:
Now try /fix on the same selection. Even if your code looks correct, Copilot will often find edge cases — empty arrays, null values, unexpected types.

Review the diff and click Apply if the fix looks good.

steps.title: Generate Tests
steps.instruction:
Select your function and type /tests in the chat. Copilot generates unit tests covering normal cases, edge cases, and error conditions.

Click Insert into New File to save them alongside your code.

steps.title: Generate Documentation
steps.instruction:
Select one of your functions and type /doc in the chat. Copilot generates a documentation comment (JSDoc, docstring, etc.) tailored to the function’s parameters and return value.

Review the output and click Apply to insert it above your function.

steps.title: Ask About Your Workspace
steps.instruction:
Now try the @workspace participant. Type:

@workspace What files are in this project and what do they do?

Unlike regular chat, @workspace indexes your entire project — not just the open file. It can answer questions about project structure, dependencies, and cross-file relationships.
steps.tip: Type <kbd>/</kbd> in the chat input to see all available commands. There are more than the ones we covered here.

steps.title: Close Chat
steps.instruction:
We’ve closed the Chat panel for you. Across these sections you opened the sidebar chat, chose a model, asked context-aware questions, used inline chat (Ctrl+I), and tried slash commands like /explain, /fix, /tests, /doc, and @workspace.

Bring Copilot Chat back any time with Ctrl+Alt+I.

steps.title: Find the Mode Selector
steps.instruction:
Look at the top of the Chat panel, next to the model picker. You’ll see a dropdown for the current mode (Ask, Edit, or Agent).

It may default to Ask. Click it to see all three options.

steps.title: Try Ask Mode
steps.instruction:
With Ask selected, type:

What does my shuffle function do and are there any edge cases I should handle?

Notice that Ask mode provides an answer but does not modify your files. This is the safest mode for exploration and learning.

steps.title: Try Edit Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch to Edit mode using the mode selector. Type:

Add JSDoc comments to all functions in my open file

Copilot proposes changes as diffs. Review each change and click Accept or Discard individually. Edit mode is ideal for targeted refactoring across one or more files.

steps.title: Try Agent Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch to Agent mode. Type:

Create a test file for the functions in my open file using a testing framework

Watch how Agent mode plans the task, may run terminal commands (like installing a test framework), creates files, and iterates if something fails. You will be asked to approve terminal commands before they run.
steps.tip: Agent mode is powerful but requires more oversight. Always review what it plans to do, especially terminal commands.

steps.title: Try Vision
steps.instruction:
Take a screenshot of anything on your screen (or use any image file). Drag and drop or paste (Ctrl+V) the image directly into the Chat input.

Type: What does this show?

Copilot analyzes the image and responds. This works with UI mockups, error screenshots, architecture diagrams, and more.

steps.title: Back to Ask Mode
steps.instruction:
Switch back to Ask mode. We’ve closed the Chat panel for you.

You’ve now used all three chat modes: Ask for exploration, Edit for targeted changes, and Agent for autonomous execution. Plus Vision for image analysis.

steps.title: Open in Editor
steps.instruction:
Look at the top of the Copilot Chat panel. Find the "Open in Editor" button (pencil icon) at the top-right of the Chat panel.

Click it. Your current chat session moves into an editor tab.

steps.title: Open a Second Chat
steps.instruction:
Now open the Chat panel again with Ctrl+Alt+I. You now have two independent sessions: one in an editor tab and one in the sidebar.

Type a different question in each one. Notice they have completely separate contexts and histories.

steps.title: Why This Matters
steps.instruction:
LLM inference is slow. Every prompt waits in a queue, then the model generates its response token by token.

With two sessions, you can ask one agent to write tests while the other explains a function. Neither blocks the other. In later courses, you will use this technique to run entire parallel workflows.
steps.tip: You can repeat this as many times as you want. Every "Open in Editor" click creates another independent context window.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab and sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Open in Editor: pencil icon at top of Chat panel
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I

steps.title: Explore Copilot Settings
steps.instruction:
We've opened Settings for you (Ctrl+,). Search for copilot to see all available options.

Find github.copilot.enable and notice it accepts per-language toggles — you can enable Copilot for Python but disable it for Markdown.

steps.title: Try a Per-Language Toggle
steps.instruction:
Click Edit in settings.json next to the github.copilot.enable setting and add:

"github.copilot.enable": { "*": true, "markdown": false }

This keeps Copilot active everywhere except Markdown files. Teams often disable it for plain text and config files where suggestions aren't helpful.
steps.tip: Put these settings in .vscode/settings.json (not your user settings) and commit the file. Everyone who clones the repo gets the same configuration.

steps.title: Preview Custom Instructions
steps.instruction:
Search for copilot instructions in Settings. You'll see the option to point Copilot at a .github/copilot-instructions.md file.

This file lets you encode team standards ("always use async/await", "prefer named exports") so Copilot follows them automatically. The next course in this track covers custom instructions in depth.

steps.title: Close Settings
steps.instruction: We've closed the Settings tab for you. Remember, you can always bring it back with Ctrl+, and search for copilot to find most Copilot-related settings.

steps.title: Open the Terminal
steps.instruction:
We've opened the integrated terminal for you (Ctrl+`).

Copilot isn't just for code files — it works in the terminal too. Try asking in the Chat panel:

@terminal how do I list all git branches, including remote ones?

steps.title: Try Inline Terminal Help
steps.instruction:
Click inside the terminal and press Ctrl+I to open inline Copilot right in the terminal.

Type: Find all JavaScript files modified in the last 7 days

Copilot will suggest the command. Press Enter to insert it into the terminal — it won't run automatically, so you can review it first.
steps.tip: Inline terminal assistance is especially helpful for complex commands with many flags (think git, docker, or kubectl).

steps.title: Ask for a Multi-Step Workflow
steps.instruction:
In the Chat panel, try a more complex request:

@terminal How do I create a new git branch, make a commit, and push it to origin?

Copilot gives you the full sequence of commands with explanations. This is great for CLI workflows you don't use every day.

steps.title: Close the Terminal
steps.instruction:
We've closed the terminal for you. You used @terminal in Chat to get CLI help, tried inline terminal suggestions with Ctrl+I, and asked for multi-step command workflows.

Toggle the terminal back any time with Ctrl+`.

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You’ve activated Copilot, explored Next Edit Suggestions, browsed the model selector, used all three chat modes (Ask, Edit, Agent), tried Vision, worked with slash commands (/explain, /fix, /tests, /doc, @workspace), used both sidebar and inline chat, worked in the terminal, and configured settings.

You’re ready for the next course: Prompts, Instructions, Skills, and Agents.
steps.tip: Head back to the slides on the left for a recap, or close this panel to explore on your own.
